mappings.reason: Requiring MFA for externally-exposed applications implements a core Zero Trust authentication mechanism for a subset of enterprise resources.
Requiring MFA for remote network access implements a core Zero Trust authentication mechanism for a subset of access types.
Requiring MFA for administrative access implements a core Zero Trust authentication mechanism for a subset of privileged users.
Centralizing access control through a directory or SSO provider establishes the foundational identity and policy architecture required for a Zero Trust model.
Centralizing Network AAA establishes the foundational authentication and authorization infrastructure required to enforce Zero Trust across the network.
Adopting secure network protocols like 802.1X enables network-level authentication and access control consistent with Zero Trust principles.
Requiring users to authenticate to VPN and authentication services prior to accessing resources directly satisfies the authentication-before-access action for remote devices.
Managing access control based on device posture (anti-malware, compliance) implements the Zero Trust requirement to verify device health prior to granting access.
Deploying port-level access control (802.1X) enforces network authentication and authorization at the connection point, aligning with Zero Trust network access.
